yaml.title: **MATH 3190 Homework 8**
yaml.subtitle:
Due April 2, 2026\
Focus: Notes 9\
Total Points: 90 + 10 (Self-Critique Comment and Uploading to GitHub) = 100

markdown.markdown:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add
your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file
should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker
to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a
pdf). You will "turn in" this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd
and output file, to your GitHub `Math_3190_Assignment` repository in the
`Homework/Homework_8` directory. Make sure all supporting files (if
there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use `git` and
`Unix` commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don't manually upload files
to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on
Canvas to unlock the solutions.

Don't forget to also put a [self-critique comment]{.underline} on Canvas
indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any
questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the
assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.


Three airlines serve a small town in Ohio. Airline A has 52% of all
scheduled flights, airline B has 35% and airline C has the remaining
13%. Their on-time rates are 85%, 67%, and 41%, respectively. A flight
just left on-time. What is the probability that it was a flight of
airline A?

60.56%

# Problem 2 (15 points)

Suppose we have a data set with each observation $x_i$ independent and
identically exponentially distributed for $i=1,2,\dots,n$. That is,
$x_i\sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ where $\lambda$ is the rate parameter. We
would like to find a posterior (or at least a function proportional to
it) for $\lambda$.

### Part a (6 points)

Write down the likelihood function (or a function proportional to it) in
this situation. We would call this $p(x|\lambda)$.

$$
p(x|\lambda)= \prod_{i=1}^{n}\lambda e^{- \lambda x_i}
$$

### Part b (6 points)

Now let $\lambda$ have a normal prior with mean 0.1 and variance 1:
$\lambda\sim N(1/10,\ 1)$. Use this and the likelihood from part (a) to
write down a function that is proportional to the posterior of $\lambda$
given $\boldsymbol{x}$. We call this $p(\lambda|\boldsymbol{x})$.

$$
p(\lambda | x) = \lambda^n  exp(- \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i)exp(-\frac{(\lambda - 0.1)^2}{2})
$$

### Part c (3 points)

Which would be more appropriate here to obtain samples of $\lambda$, the
Gibbs or Metropolis algorithm? Explain why. You may want to look on page
171 of Notes 9 in the conjugate prior table.

The Metropolis algorithm only requires a function proportional to the
posterior, which I have.

# Problem 3 (30 points)

Suppose we have the vector
`x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)`
that we believe comes from a gamma distribution with shape of 10 and
some rate $\beta$: $x_i\sim\text{Gam}(10,\ \beta)$. We will use sampling
to obtain some information about $\beta$. Let’s put a gamma prior on
$\beta$ with a shape of $\alpha_0$ and a rate of 1:
$\beta\sim\text{Gam}(\alpha_0,\ 1)$.

### Part a (5 points)

Use the fact that this is a conjugate prior to write down what kind of
distribution the posterior of $\beta$, which is
$p(\beta|\boldsymbol{x})$, is.

$$
\beta | x ~ Gamma(\alpha_0+10n, 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i) 
$$

### Part b (5 points)

Let $\alpha_0=1$. In an **R** code chunk, sample 10,000 $\beta$ values
from the distribution you wrote down in part (a) using the `rgamma()`
function and report the 95% credible interval for $\beta$ using the
2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

```{r}
#| label: 2b

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 1

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

(4.07, 5.67)

### Part c (3 points)

Repeat part (b) with $\alpha_0 = 10$.

```{r}
#| label: 2c

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 10

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

(4.35, 6.00)

### Part d (3 points)

Repeat part (b) with $\alpha_0 = 100$.

```{r}
#| label: 2d

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 100

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

7.22, 9.30

### Part e (4 points)

Comment on the effect the value of $\alpha_0$ has on the credible
intervals for $\beta$ as revealed in parts (b), (c), and (d).

The credible intervals got bigger as the value of $\alpha_0$ increased.

### Part f (6 points)

Now suppose we have twice as much data (given in the **R** code chunk
below). Repeat parts (b), (c), and (d) using this `x` vector given below
instead of the other one and report the three 95% credible intervals.
Note, this new vector x will change the shape and rate parameters used
in the `rgamma()` functions.

```{r}
#| label: new_x
x <- c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54,
       2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78, 2.76, 1.70, 1.42, 2.16, 1.07, 2.21,
       2.38, 2.27, 1.72, 1.44, 1.54, 1.72, 1.87, 1.39)
```

### Part g (4 points)

In this problem, the true $\beta$ value is 5. Write a sentence or two
about the effect adding more data has to these credible intervals by
comparing the intervals from parts (b)-(d) to the intervals from part
(f).

```{r}

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 10

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

The credible intervals are smaller when adding more data.

# Problem 4 (35 points)

Let’s apply the Bayesian framework to a regression problem. In the
`math3190package` library is the `treeseeds` dataset (it is also on
GitHub) that contains information about species of tree, the count of
seeds it produces, and the average weight of those seeds in mg.

### Part a (3 points)

Using the `treeseeds` data, create new variables defined by taking the
log of the counts and weights. Fit an OLS regression model using logged
weight to predict logged count.

```{r}
#| label: treeseeds
library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
tree <- treeseeds |>
  mutate(lcount = log(count),
         lweight = log(weight))

# Fit model here
mod = lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree)
```

### Part b (5 points)

Please read this carefully and try to understand what is being done
here. There is just one question at the end of this part for you. We
will walk through the mathematics and code of obtaining the posterior
and samples together here since this problem will focus on looking at
diagnostics, but I want you to see the **Metropolis algorithm** in
action! Make sure to read this in the html or pdf file where all
equations are rendered nicely.

Assuming the true errors are normal with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^2$,
$\epsilon_i\sim N(0,\ \sigma^2)$, it can be shown that each $y_i$ has
the distribution $$
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
$$

So, we can write the likelihood is $$
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2)\propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
$$ where $y_i$ is the log(count) for observation $i$ and $x_i$ is the
log(weight) for observation $i$. Note that here we think of
$\boldsymbol{y}$ as being random and $\boldsymbol{x}$ as being fixed. We
could, in theory, think of the vector $\boldsymbol{x}$ as also being
random and put a prior on it. But we won’t do that here.

Now, let’s just put uniform priors on $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ so the
priors are proportional to 1. Also, let’s assume $\sigma^2=1$. This
seems reasonable since $s_e^2$, the MSE, is 0.877. Of course, we could
put a prior on $\sigma^2$ as well and sample it too, but we will focus
on only sampling $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.

Now, with those uniform priors, and plugging in 1 for $\sigma^2$, we
have that the joint posterior of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ is: $$
p(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}) \propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)=f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}).
$$

Then, we can take the log to get $$
\ln(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}))=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2.
$$

Our goal now is to obtain samples of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. Let’s use
the Metropolis algorithm to do this. Using the log of the function
proportional to the joint posterior of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$,
$\ln\Big(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y})\Big)$, we'll
write a Metropolis algorithm in **R**. For $\beta_0$, we will use a
normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value,
$\beta_0^{(i)}$, with a standard deviation of 0.8 and for $\beta_1$, and
we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous
value, $\beta_1^{(i)}$, with a standard deviation of 0.1. The starting
values don’t matter too much, but we'll use $\beta_0^{(0)}=10$ and
$\beta_1^{(0)}=-0.5$.

This code will generate 10,000 samples and plot the chains for $\beta_0$
and $\beta_1$. In addition, the 95% credible interval for $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$ are given.

```{r}
#| label: mcmc
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8
library(patchwork)
set.seed(2026)
nsamps <- 10000
beta0 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta1 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta0[1] <- 10
beta1[1] <- -0.5
for (i in 1:(nsamps - 1)) {
  beta0_star <- rnorm(1, beta0[i], 0.8) # Proposed new value for intercept
  beta1_star <- rnorm(1, beta1[i], 0.1) # Proposed new value for slope
  # y is tree$lcount and x is tree$lweight
  lf1 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0_star - beta1_star * tree$lweight)^2)
  lf2 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0[i] - beta1[i] * tree$lweight)^2)
  if (log(runif(1)) < lf1 - lf2) {
    # Accept the proposed values as new sample values if the above 
    #   condition is satisfied
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0_star
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1_star
  } else {
    # Otherwise set the new sample values to be equal to the previous ones
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0[i]
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1[i]
  }
}

mcmc_df <- data.frame(index = 1:nsamps, beta0 = beta0, beta1 = beta1)
mcmc_plot1 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta0)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(8, 11, by = 0.5)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[0]))

mcmc_plot2 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta1)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-0.75, -0.25, by = 0.1)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[1]))

mcmc_plot1 / mcmc_plot2

quantile(beta0, c(.025, .975))
quantile(beta1, c(.025, .975))
```

The plots of the chains are given in the output above.

The 95% credible interval for $\beta_0$ is $(8.53, 10.21)$.

The 95% credible interval for $\beta_1$ is $(-0.661, -0.368)$.

Your intervals may differ a bit due to the random sampling.

Okay, here is the question for you! Based on the plots of the chains
above, does it look like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well?
Explain.

### Part c (3 points)

Interpret both of the credible intervals from part (b).

There is a 95% chance that $\beta_0$ lies between 8.53 and 10.21

There is a 95% chance that $\beta_1$ lies between -0.661 and -0.368

### Part d (5 points)

Find and report the integrated autocorrelation time for the $\beta_0$
and $\beta_1$ chains. Each chain will have their own $\hat{\tau}_{int}$
value, so you should report two (although they will be similar).

```{r}
1 + 2 * sum(abs(acf(beta0, lag.max = 100, plot = F)$acf))
1 + 2 * sum(abs(acf(beta1, lag.max = 100, plot = F)$acf))
```

$\beta_0$: 25.73

$\beta_1$: 26.62

### Part e (3 points)

Based on the integrated autocorrelation time for the $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$ chains, how many MCMC samples would you need to generate to
get the equivalent of 10,000 independent samples?

370 MCMC samples

### Part f (3 points)

Let’s compare these credible intervals to some other intervals. First,
obtain the 95% $t$ confidence intervals for $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ just
using the `confint()` function on the model you fit in part (a) and
report them here.

```{r}
confint(mod)
```

$\beta_0$: (8.54, 10.22)

$\beta_1$: (-0.67, -0.36)

### Part g (8 points)

Now let's obtain confidence intervals using bootstrapping the cases like
we did with in Notes 7 and HW 5. You can use the `bootstrapping()`
function in the `math3190package` for this or do it yourself with a
loop. Set a seed and then using at least 10,000 bootstrap samples,
report the 95% percentile confidence intervals for $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$.

```{r}
library(math3190package)
set.seed(7)

bootcases = function(resids, i){
  lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree[i,])$coef
}
boot = bootstrapping(residuals(mod), bootcases, 5000)
boot_ci(boot)
```

$\beta_0$: (8.13, 10.1)

$\beta_1$: (-0.645, -0.324)

### Part i (5 points)

Write a couple sentences comparing all of the intervals in parts (b),
(f), and (g).

Part b and f have almost the same intervals. The bootstrapped confidence
intervals are slightly smaller than the other two.

# Problem 1 (10 points)

Three airlines serve a small town in Ohio. Airline A has 52% of all
scheduled flights, airline B has 35% and airline C has the remaining
13%. Their on-time rates are 85%, 67%, and 41%, respectively. A flight
just left on-time. What is the probability that it was a flight of
airline A?

60.56%

# Problem 2 (15 points)

Suppose we have a data set with each observation $x_i$ independent and
identically exponentially distributed for $i=1,2,\dots,n$. That is,
$x_i\sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ where $\lambda$ is the rate parameter. We
would like to find a posterior (or at least a function proportional to
it) for $\lambda$.

### Part a (6 points)

Write down the likelihood function (or a function proportional to it) in
this situation. We would call this $p(x|\lambda)$.

$$
p(x|\lambda)= \prod_{i=1}^{n}\lambda e^{- \lambda x_i}
$$

### Part b (6 points)

Now let $\lambda$ have a normal prior with mean 0.1 and variance 1:
$\lambda\sim N(1/10,\ 1)$. Use this and the likelihood from part (a) to
write down a function that is proportional to the posterior of $\lambda$
given $\boldsymbol{x}$. We call this $p(\lambda|\boldsymbol{x})$.

$$
p(\lambda | x) = \lambda^n  exp(- \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i)exp(-\frac{(\lambda - 0.1)^2}{2})
$$

### Part c (3 points)

Which would be more appropriate here to obtain samples of $\lambda$, the
Gibbs or Metropolis algorithm? Explain why. You may want to look on page
171 of Notes 9 in the conjugate prior table.

The Metropolis algorithm only requires a function proportional to the
posterior, which I have.

# Problem 3 (30 points)

Suppose we have the vector
`x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)`
that we believe comes from a gamma distribution with shape of 10 and
some rate $\beta$: $x_i\sim\text{Gam}(10,\ \beta)$. We will use sampling
to obtain some information about $\beta$. Let’s put a gamma prior on
$\beta$ with a shape of $\alpha_0$ and a rate of 1:
$\beta\sim\text{Gam}(\alpha_0,\ 1)$.

### Part a (5 points)

Use the fact that this is a conjugate prior to write down what kind of
distribution the posterior of $\beta$, which is
$p(\beta|\boldsymbol{x})$, is.

$$
\beta | x ~ Gamma(\alpha_0+10n, 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i) 
$$

### Part b (5 points)

Let $\alpha_0=1$. In an **R** code chunk, sample 10,000 $\beta$ values
from the distribution you wrote down in part (a) using the `rgamma()`
function and report the 95% credible interval for $\beta$ using the
2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

```{r}
#| label: 2b

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 1

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

(4.07, 5.67)

### Part c (3 points)

Repeat part (b) with $\alpha_0 = 10$.

```{r}
#| label: 2c

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 10

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

(4.35, 6.00)

### Part d (3 points)

Repeat part (b) with $\alpha_0 = 100$.

```{r}
#| label: 2d

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 100

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

7.22, 9.30

### Part e (4 points)

Comment on the effect the value of $\alpha_0$ has on the credible
intervals for $\beta$ as revealed in parts (b), (c), and (d).

The credible intervals got bigger as the value of $\alpha_0$ increased.

### Part f (6 points)

Now suppose we have twice as much data (given in the **R** code chunk
below). Repeat parts (b), (c), and (d) using this `x` vector given below
instead of the other one and report the three 95% credible intervals.
Note, this new vector x will change the shape and rate parameters used
in the `rgamma()` functions.

```{r}
#| label: new_x
x <- c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54,
       2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78, 2.76, 1.70, 1.42, 2.16, 1.07, 2.21,
       2.38, 2.27, 1.72, 1.44, 1.54, 1.72, 1.87, 1.39)
```

### Part g (4 points)

In this problem, the true $\beta$ value is 5. Write a sentence or two
about the effect adding more data has to these credible intervals by
comparing the intervals from parts (b)-(d) to the intervals from part
(f).

```{r}

n = length(x)
alpha0 = 10

shape = alpha0 + 10 *n
rate = 1 + sum(x)

set.seed(7)
beta = rgamma(10000, shape = shape, rate=rate)
quantile(beta, c(0.025, 0.975))
```

The credible intervals are smaller when adding more data.

# Problem 4 (35 points)

Let’s apply the Bayesian framework to a regression problem. In the
`math3190package` library is the `treeseeds` dataset (it is also on
GitHub) that contains information about species of tree, the count of
seeds it produces, and the average weight of those seeds in mg.

### Part a (3 points)

Using the `treeseeds` data, create new variables defined by taking the
log of the counts and weights. Fit an OLS regression model using logged
weight to predict logged count.

```{r}
#| label: treeseeds
library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
tree <- treeseeds |>
  mutate(lcount = log(count),
         lweight = log(weight))

# Fit model here
mod = lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree)
```

### Part b (5 points)

Please read this carefully and try to understand what is being done

and samples together here since this problem will focus on looking at

p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(

p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2)\propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)

priors are proportional to 1. Also, let’s assume $\sigma^2=1$. This
seems reasonable since $s_e^2$, the MSE, is 0.877. Of course, we could

Now, with those uniform priors, and plugging in 1 for $\sigma^2$, we

p(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}) \propto\exp\left(

$$

Then, we can take the log to get $$
\ln(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}))=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2.
$$

Our goal now is to obtain samples of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. Let’s use
the Metropolis algorithm to do this. Using the log of the function
proportional to the joint posterior of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$,
$\ln\Big(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y})\Big)$, we'll
write a Metropolis algorithm in **R**. For $\beta_0$, we will use a

$\beta_0^{(i)}$, with a standard deviation of 0.8 and for $\beta_1$, and
we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous
value, $\beta_1^{(i)}$, with a standard deviation of 0.1. The starting
values don’t matter too much, but we'll use $\beta_0^{(0)}=10$ and
$\beta_1^{(0)}=-0.5$.

This code will generate 10,000 samples and plot the chains for $\beta_0$
and $\beta_1$. In addition, the 95% credible interval for $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$ are given.

```{r}
#| label: mcmc
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8
library(patchwork)
set.seed(2026)
nsamps <- 10000
beta0 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta1 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta0[1] <- 10
beta1[1] <- -0.5
for (i in 1:(nsamps - 1)) {
  beta0_star <- rnorm(1, beta0[i], 0.8) # Proposed new value for intercept
  beta1_star <- rnorm(1, beta1[i], 0.1) # Proposed new value for slope
  # y is tree$lcount and x is tree$lweight
  lf1 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0_star - beta1_star * tree$lweight)^2)
  lf2 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0[i] - beta1[i] * tree$lweight)^2)
  if (log(runif(1)) < lf1 - lf2) {
    # Accept the proposed values as new sample values if the above 
    #   condition is satisfied
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0_star
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1_star
  } else {
    # Otherwise set the new sample values to be equal to the previous ones
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0[i]
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1[i]
  }
}

mcmc_df <- data.frame(index = 1:nsamps, beta0 = beta0, beta1 = beta1)
mcmc_plot1 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta0)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(8, 11, by = 0.5)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[0]))

mcmc_plot2 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta1)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-0.75, -0.25, by = 0.1)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[1]))

mcmc_plot1 / mcmc_plot2

quantile(beta0, c(.025, .975))
quantile(beta1, c(.025, .975))
```

The plots of the chains are given in the output above.

The 95% credible interval for $\beta_0$ is $(8.53, 10.21)$.

The 95% credible interval for $\beta_1$ is $(-0.661, -0.368)$.

Your intervals may differ a bit due to the random sampling.

Okay, here is the question for you! Based on the plots of the chains
above, does it look like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well?
Explain.

### Part c (3 points)

Interpret both of the credible intervals from part (b).

There is a 95% chance that $\beta_0$ lies between 8.53 and 10.21

There is a 95% chance that $\beta_1$ lies between -0.661 and -0.368

### Part d (5 points)

Find and report the integrated autocorrelation time for the $\beta_0$
and $\beta_1$ chains. Each chain will have their own $\hat{\tau}_{int}$
value, so you should report two (although they will be similar).

```{r}
1 + 2 * sum(abs(acf(beta0, lag.max = 100, plot = F)$acf))
1 + 2 * sum(abs(acf(beta1, lag.max = 100, plot = F)$acf))
```

$\beta_0$: 25.73

$\beta_1$: 26.62

### Part e (3 points)

Based on the integrated autocorrelation time for the $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$ chains, how many MCMC samples would you need to generate to
get the equivalent of 10,000 independent samples?

370 MCMC samples

### Part f (3 points)

Let’s compare these credible intervals to some other intervals. First,
obtain the 95% $t$ confidence intervals for $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ just
using the `confint()` function on the model you fit in part (a) and
report them here.

```{r}
confint(mod)
```

$\beta_0$: (8.54, 10.22)

$\beta_1$: (-0.67, -0.36)

### Part g (8 points)

Now let's obtain confidence intervals using bootstrapping the cases like
we did with in Notes 7 and HW 5. You can use the `bootstrapping()`
function in the `math3190package` for this or do it yourself with a
loop. Set a seed and then using at least 10,000 bootstrap samples,
report the 95% percentile confidence intervals for $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$.

```{r}
library(math3190package)
set.seed(7)

bootcases = function(resids, i){
  lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree[i,])$coef
}
boot = bootstrapping(residuals(mod), bootcases, 5000)
boot_ci(boot)
```

$\beta_0$: (8.13, 10.1)

$\beta_1$: (-0.645, -0.324)

### Part i (5 points)

Write a couple sentences comparing all of the intervals in parts (b),
(f), and (g).

Part b and f have almost the same intervals. The bootstrapped confidence
intervals are slightly smaller than the other two.
